article_title: Claude Desktop changes software permissions without consent
core_thesis: Anthropic's Claude Desktop for macOS silently installs Native Messaging manifest files that pre-authorize browser extensions across multiple Chromium-based browsers—including browsers not yet installed—without user consent or disclosure. Privacy consultant Alexander Hanff argues this violates EU ePrivacy Directive Article 5(3) and constitutes a dark pattern, while security experts note it expands the attack surface by creating a persistent, pre-authorized bridge from browser extensions to a local executable running outside the browser sandbox.
detailed_summary: The article reports that Claude Desktop for macOS writes a Native Messaging manifest file (com.anthropic.claude_browser_extension.json) to multiple Chromium-based browser directories (e.g., Chrome, Edge, Brave) during installation. This file pre-authorizes three Chrome extension IDs, including the 'Claude in Chrome' extension, allowing those extensions to communicate with a local binary via the Native Messaging API. Crucially, this occurs even for browsers not yet installed on the user's system, meaning future browser installations will automatically grant Claude access without any user prompt. Alexander Hanff discovered this while debugging another application and claims he never installed any Anthropic browser extensions. He argues this is a breach of Article 5(3) of the ePrivacy Directive, which requires explicit consent before storing information on a user's device. Security expert Noah M. Kenney agrees the behavior is technically reproducible and likely falls outside the 'strictly necessary' exemption under EU law, but pushes back on the term 'spyware' since the integration layer remains dormant until triggered by a browser extension. However, he emphasizes that the pre-positioned bridge meaningfully expands the attack surface, especially given that Claude for Chrome has a 23.6% prompt injection success rate (11.2% with mitigations), which could allow attackers to escape the browser sandbox via the Native Messaging host. Anthropic did not respond to requests for comment, and a related bug in the native messaging host registration was auto-closed without fix.

entities.name: Alexander Hanff
entities.role: Privacy consultant who discovered and reported the Claude Desktop behavior, arguing it violates EU privacy law.

entities.name: Noah M. Kenney
entities.role: Founder of Digital 520, security consultant who provides technical and legal analysis of the issue.

entities.name: Anthropic
entities.role: AI lab that developed Claude Desktop; the subject of the privacy/security allegations.

entities.name: Claude Desktop
entities.role: macOS desktop application that silently installs Native Messaging manifests for browser integration.

entities.name: Claude in Chrome
entities.role: Browser extension pre-authorized by the manifest; vulnerable to prompt injection.

entities.name: Article 5(3) of Directive 2002/58/EC
entities.role: EU ePrivacy Directive requiring consent before storing/accessing information on a user's device.

entities.name: Native Messaging API
entities.role: API allowing browser extensions to communicate with native executables; used by Claude Desktop to bridge browser and local binary.

key_data_points: Claude Desktop writes a Native Messaging manifest file (com.anthropic.claude_browser_extension.json) to multiple Chromium-based browser directories during installation, pre-authorizing three Chrome extension IDs without user consent. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: This creates a persistent, pre-authorized bridge that could be exploited for privilege escalation or lateral movement if the browser extension is compromised.)

The manifest file is written even for browsers not yet installed on the user's system, meaning future browser installations will automatically grant Claude access without any prompt. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: Attackers could leverage this pre-positioned integration to establish persistence or execute commands outside the browser sandbox.)

Claude for Chrome has a 23.6% prompt injection success rate without mitigations and 11.2% with current mitigations, according to Anthropic's own safety data. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: Prompt injection can be used to hijack the extension and, via the Native Messaging bridge, execute arbitrary code on the host at user privilege level.)

The Native Messaging binary runs outside the browser sandbox at user privilege level, without surfacing any permission prompts. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: This bypasses browser sandbox protections, allowing an attacker who compromises the extension to gain code execution on the host.)

Anthropic's approach is invisible by default, with no opt-in, difficult to remove, and pre-authorizes extensions that haven't been installed. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: The stealthy installation and persistence make it an attractive vector for supply chain or initial access attacks.)

A related bug in the native messaging host registration (Claude Code vs. Claude Desktop conflict) was auto-closed by a GitHub Actions bot without being fixed. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: Unfixed bugs in the integration layer could be exploited for denial of service or privilege escalation.)
conclusions: Claude Desktop's silent installation of Native Messaging manifests constitutes a dark pattern and likely violates EU ePrivacy Directive Article 5(3) because it stores information on the user's device without explicit consent and is not strictly necessary for the service the user requested. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: This legal vulnerability could be used to pressure Anthropic into changing the behavior, but from an offensive perspective, the current design provides a reliable, pre-authorized bridge for exploitation.)

The pre-positioned integration layer expands the attack surface by creating a persistent, pre-authorized bridge from browser extensions to a local executable running outside the browser sandbox. Combined with the high prompt injection success rate, this presents a realistic attack path for compromising user systems. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: Red teamers can weaponize prompt injection against Claude for Chrome to achieve host code execution via the Native Messaging bridge.)

Even if the behavior is not technically 'spyware' (since it remains dormant until triggered), it breaks the widely understood trust boundary that desktop applications should not silently modify other applications. This erodes user trust and exposes Anthropic to regulatory and reputational risk. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: The trust boundary violation can be used in social engineering or phishing scenarios to convince users that Claude Desktop is unsafe, or alternatively, to demonstrate the risks of AI integration.)
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your Red Teaming and cybersecurity interests because it details a real-world, exploitable attack surface created by an AI desktop application. The silent installation of Native Messaging manifests provides a persistent, pre-authorized bridge from browser extensions to a local executable outside the sandbox, which can be leveraged via prompt injection to achieve host code execution. As a programmer, you can analyze the Native Messaging manifest structure and the binary's behavior to develop proof-of-concept exploits or detection rules. The article also highlights legal and regulatory angles (EU ePrivacy Directive) that could inform your understanding of consent and trust boundaries in software. For your homelab, you can test this behavior in a controlled environment to reproduce the findings and experiment with mitigation strategies.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a macOS VM with Claude Desktop installed and use a debugger or sysmon-like tool to monitor file writes to browser directories. Then attempt to exploit the prompt injection vulnerability in Claude for Chrome to execute arbitrary commands via the Native Messaging bridge. This would make an excellent red team exercise or CTF challenge. Additionally, you could write a detection rule (e.g., for osquery or Sigma) that alerts when unexpected Native Messaging manifests appear in browser directories.
